# ARTICLE 1

**Title:** i deployed 122 websites in 6 weeks with zero hosting costs. here's how.

**Tags:** `webdev` `javascript` `pwa` `deployment`

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Cover image alt:** terminal showing surge deploy command, 122 sites live

---

i deployed 122 websites in 6 weeks. $0 hosting cost. one command per site. here's the exact setup.

no server bills. no CI/CD pipeline. no AWS console. just `surge .` and the site is live in under 30 seconds.

## what i built

- 13 streak tracking apps (scripture-streak, fitness-streak, coding-streak, meditation-streak, and 9 others)
- 10 SaaS tools
- 7 local business demo sites
- 5 faith apps including Ramadan-specific tools
- 5 productivity apps
- geo-targeted landing pages

live examples right now: [prayerlock-app.surge.sh](https://prayerlock-app.surge.sh), [coldmaxx-app.surge.sh](https://coldmaxx-app.surge.sh), [pagescorer.surge.sh](https://pagescorer.surge.sh), [sitescore-app.surge.sh](https://sitescore-app.surge.sh).

go check them. they load.

## why surge.sh

surge is free static hosting. no account required beyond email signup. no dashboard to navigate. no billing page.

the deploy command is literally:

```bash
surge .
```

that's it. you run that from your project folder and it's live. it asks for an email and password the first time, then never again.

if you want a custom subdomain:

```bash
surge . myapp-name.surge.sh
```

done. live. indexed by Google within 48 hours.

the free tier has no site limit. i confirmed this at 40 sites, at 80 sites, and again at 122.

## the stack that made this possible

i didn't use any framework. no React, no Next.js, no build step.

the stack per site:

- vanilla HTML
- vanilla CSS (CSS custom properties for theming)
- vanilla JS (no bundler)
- a PWA service worker (offline support + installability)
- localStorage for all data persistence

that combination means every site is a single folder. to deploy, you `cd` into it and run `surge .`. no build command. no `npm install`. nothing.

each site is between 45KB and 80KB total. they load in under 1 second on a 3G connection.

## the deploy flow at scale

when you have 122 sites, you need a smarter process than manually typing surge commands. here's the actual script:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# deploy-all.sh
# runs from parent directory containing all site folders

for dir in */; do
  if [ -f "$dir/index.html" ]; then
    site_name=$(basename "$dir")
    echo "deploying: $site_name"
    cd "$dir"
    surge . "${site_name}-app.surge.sh" --token $SURGE_TOKEN
    cd ..
    echo "live: https://${site_name}-app.surge.sh"
  fi
done
```

set `SURGE_TOKEN` once:

```bash
surge token
# copy the output
export SURGE_TOKEN=your_token_here
```

then `./deploy-all.sh` and walk away. all 122 sites deploy in about 4 minutes.

## what about updates

updates are the same command. surge overwrites the previous deploy. no versioning, no rollback built-in. for static sites that update rarely, this is fine.

if you need rollbacks, keep your folders in git. rolling back is `git checkout [commit]` then `surge .` again. takes 35 seconds.

## the actual tradeoffs

i want to be honest about what surge doesn't give you:

no server-side rendering. if your site needs a database or user auth, surge is the wrong tool. surge is for static HTML, CSS, JS, and assets only.

no custom domain on the free tier. you get `.surge.sh` subdomains. for demos and tools, this is fine. for a business's main site, pay for a domain and point it at surge with their paid plan ($30/year).

no analytics built in. i use a small JS snippet that calls a free Plausible endpoint or just reads from the browser's own performance API.

no SSL on custom domains unless you upgrade. the `.surge.sh` subdomains get SSL for free.

## the actual numbers

- 122 sites deployed: 6 weeks
- average deploy time: 28 seconds per site
- hosting cost: $0
- total time spent on deployment infrastructure: maybe 3 hours across the whole 6 weeks
- time saved vs. managing even a single VPS: significant

most developers spend more time configuring Nginx than i spent deploying all 122 sites combined.

## who this is for

if you're prototyping SaaS tools, building demo sites, shipping PWAs, or testing landing pages, surge is the right tool.

if you're building the next Figma, it's not.

stop overthinking static hosting. just use surge.

---

# ARTICLE 2

**Title:** the PWA factory pattern: how I templated 13 apps from 1 codebase

**Tags:** `pwa` `javascript` `webdev` `productivity`

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Cover image alt:** side-by-side screenshots of scripture-streak and fitness-streak showing identical structure, different branding

---

i built 13 installable apps from a single codebase. each one took less than 20 minutes to ship. the pattern is simple enough that i'm going to hand it to you in full right now.

## the core insight

streak mechanics are universal. the user opens the app. they tap a button. they see a number go up. they come back tomorrow.

the content context is what changes. scripture reading vs. gym sessions vs. coding practice vs. Quran memorization. the underlying UX is identical.

so i built it once and swapped the context.

## what "factory" means here

factory doesn't mean a code generator or a CLI tool. it means a base template folder with specific configuration points. when i want a new app, i copy the folder, edit 3 files, and deploy.

the 13 apps i shipped this way:

- scripture-streak (Bible reading)
- fitness-streak (gym sessions)
- coding-streak (daily practice)
- meditation-streak (mindfulness sessions)
- quran-streak (Quran reading)
- torah-streak (Torah study)
- gita-streak (Bhagavad Gita study)
- buddhist-streak (meditation/sutras)
- language-streak (daily language practice)
- reading-streak (book reading)
- journal-streak (daily journaling)
- art-streak (daily creative practice)
- sikh-streak (Nitnem prayer)

13 apps. 1 base codebase. each deployed at its own surge.sh URL.

## the base template structure

```
base-template/
  index.html
  style.css
  app.js
  sw.js           (service worker)
  manifest.json
  config.js       (the only file that changes per app)
  icons/
    icon-192.png
    icon-512.png
```

every app is 7 files. that's the whole thing.

## config.js is the entire customization layer

```javascript
// config.js
const APP_CONFIG = {
  name: "Scripture Streak",
  shortName: "ScriptureStreak",
  tagline: "Daily Bible reading. One chapter at a time.",
  primaryColor: "#2D5016",
  accentColor: "#7CB518",
  actionLabel: "Mark today's reading complete",
  completionMessage: "You read today. Keep the streak going.",
  streakUnit: "days",
  theme: "faith",
  shareText: "Day {streak} of daily scripture reading."
};
```

to ship fitness-streak, i change `name`, `tagline`, `primaryColor`, `actionLabel`, and `completionMessage`. that's the entire diff.

## how the service worker enables installation

a PWA needs 3 things to be installable on mobile: HTTPS, a manifest.json, and a service worker. surge.sh gives you HTTPS for free on `.surge.sh` subdomains.

the manifest.json pulls from config.js at build time (or at runtime if you generate it dynamically):

```json
{
  "name": "Scripture Streak",
  "short_name": "ScriptureStreak",
  "start_url": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#FFFFFF",
  "theme_color": "#2D5016",
  "icons": [
    { "src": "icons/icon-192.png", "sizes": "192x192", "type": "image/png" },
    { "src": "icons/icon-512.png", "sizes": "512x512", "type": "image/png" }
  ]
}
```

the service worker caches everything on first load. after that, the app works completely offline:

```javascript
// sw.js
const CACHE_NAME = 'streak-v1';
const ASSETS = ['/', '/index.html', '/style.css', '/app.js', '/config.js', '/manifest.json'];

self.addEventListener('install', event => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.open(CACHE_NAME).then(cache => cache.addAll(ASSETS))
  );
});

self.addEventListener('fetch', event => {
  event.respondWith(
    caches.match(event.request).then(response => response || fetch(event.request))
  );
});
```

that's a 15-line service worker. it's not production-grade cache invalidation. it works.

## localStorage for all data

no backend. no database. no user accounts.

the streak data lives in the browser:

```javascript
// app.js (simplified)
function getStreak() {
  return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('streakData')) || {
    count: 0,
    lastCompletedDate: null,
    history: []
  };
}

function markComplete() {
  const data = getStreak();
  const today = new Date().toDateString();

  if (data.lastCompletedDate === today) return; // already done today

  data.count += 1;
  data.lastCompletedDate = today;
  data.history.push(today);
  localStorage.setItem('streakData', JSON.stringify(data));
  renderStreak(data);
}
```

the tradeoff: data doesn't sync across devices. if someone uninstalls the app, they lose their streak. for a free tool, this is acceptable. users who care about their data know to export it.

i added a JSON export button. 10 lines of code:

```javascript
function exportData() {
  const data = getStreak();
  const blob = new Blob([JSON.stringify(data, null, 2)], { type: 'application/json' });
  const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
  const a = document.createElement('a');
  a.href = url;
  a.download = 'streak-backup.json';
  a.click();
}
```

## the CSS theming system

one stylesheet serves all 13 apps. the config.js sets CSS custom properties at runtime:

```javascript
// in app.js, runs on load
document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--primary', APP_CONFIG.primaryColor);
document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--accent', APP_CONFIG.accentColor);
```

then the CSS just uses `var(--primary)` everywhere. swap the colors in config.js and the whole app rebrands.

## the actual time per app

i timed myself on app 9 (gita-streak):

- copy base-template folder: 8 seconds
- edit config.js: 4 minutes
- swap icons (i keep a folder of pre-made icon sets by theme): 2 minutes
- deploy to surge.sh: 28 seconds
- spot-check on mobile: 3 minutes

total: under 10 minutes.

the first app took 3 days to build the base template. every app after that took under 20 minutes. that math makes sense. do it once, copy forever.

## what didn't work

i tried using a single HTML file with JS-driven template rendering. it works technically but it breaks the service worker caching in subtle ways. separate files per app is cleaner.

i also tried sharing one service worker across all 13 apps via a CDN URL. this caused cache conflicts when two apps were installed on the same device. separate sw.js per app, even if identical, is the right call.

## ship the 14th one yourself

the template is the pattern. if you want to build your own streak app:

1. pick a context (habit, skill, practice)
2. copy this structure
3. edit config.js
4. make or find 2 icons at 192x192 and 512x512
5. `surge . your-app-name.surge.sh`

it's live. stop waiting.

---

# ARTICLE 3

**Title:** building a free website audit tool that competes with $99/month SaaS

**Tags:** `webdev` `saas` `javascript` `seo`

**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)

**Cover image alt:** side-by-side UI comparison of pagescorer.surge.sh vs. GTmetrix pricing page

---

GTmetrix charges $15/month. Ahrefs charges $99/month. SEMrush charges $120/month. i built the same core audit functionality for $0 and put it at a public URL anyone can use.

here's why that's possible and how i did it.

## the tools

three tools i've shipped in this category:

- [sitescore-app.surge.sh](https://sitescore-app.surge.sh) — overall site quality score
- [pagescorer.surge.sh](https://pagescorer.surge.sh) — page-level performance audit
- [shopmetrics-pro.surge.sh](https://shopmetrics-pro.surge.sh) — ecommerce-specific metrics

all three run entirely in the browser. no backend. no server. no database. no API keys required from the user.

## why most SaaS audit tools cost money

break it down. the cost isn't the analysis logic. the cost is:

1. server infrastructure to run the analysis at scale
2. support staff when users get confused
3. storing historical data so you can show trends over time
4. the sales team and marketing that convinced you to pay

remove all four and you have a free tool.

removing the server means running the analysis in the browser. removing history means no database. removing support means building clear UI. removing the sales layer means putting it on surge.sh and posting about it.

## the actual APIs (both free)

two APIs do the heavy lifting, both free:

**Google PageSpeed Insights API**
- free, no auth required for basic usage
- returns Lighthouse scores: Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, SEO
- returns Core Web Vitals: LCP, FID, CLS, TTFB
- 25,000 requests/day on the no-auth tier

```javascript
async function runPageSpeedAudit(url) {
  const apiUrl = `https://www.googleapis.com/pagespeedonline/v5/runPagespeed?url=${encodeURIComponent(url)}&strategy=mobile`;
  const response = await fetch(apiUrl);
  const data = await response.json();
  return {
    performance: data.lighthouseResult.categories.performance.score * 100,
    accessibility: data.lighthouseResult.categories.accessibility.score * 100,
    bestPractices: data.lighthouseResult.categories['best-practices'].score * 100,
    seo: data.lighthouseResult.categories.seo.score * 100,
    lcp: data.lighthouseResult.audits['largest-contentful-paint'].displayValue,
    cls: data.lighthouseResult.audits['cumulative-layout-shift'].displayValue,
    ttfb: data.lighthouseResult.audits['server-response-time'].displayValue
  };
}
```

**Lighthouse API (via PageSpeed)**
Google runs Lighthouse server-side and returns the full results. you get the same data as running `lighthouse` CLI locally, but you don't need Node.js or a server.

## client-side analysis that doesn't need an API

some checks run directly in the browser without any external call:

```javascript
async function analyzeOnPage(url) {
  // fetch the HTML via a CORS proxy for external URLs
  // or parse directly if same-origin
  const response = await fetch(`https://api.allorigins.win/get?url=${encodeURIComponent(url)}`);
  const data = await response.json();
  const parser = new DOMParser();
  const doc = parser.parseFromString(data.contents, 'text/html');

  return {
    titleLength: doc.title.length,
    hasMetaDescription: !!doc.querySelector('meta[name="description"]'),
    metaDescriptionLength: doc.querySelector('meta[name="description"]')?.getAttribute('content')?.length || 0,
    h1Count: doc.querySelectorAll('h1').length,
    imagesMissingAlt: doc.querySelectorAll('img:not([alt])').length,
    hasCanonical: !!doc.querySelector('link[rel="canonical"]'),
    internalLinkCount: doc.querySelectorAll('a[href^="/"]').length,
    wordCount: doc.body.innerText.split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean).length
  };
}
```

that's 12 real SEO checks with zero server cost.

## the competitor comparison

here's what you actually get vs. what you pay:

| Tool | Monthly cost | Lighthouse scores | Core Web Vitals | On-page SEO | Historical data |
|------|-------------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|----------------|
| GTmetrix | $15 | Yes | Yes | Partial | Yes |
| Ahrefs Site Audit | $99 | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| SEMrush | $120 | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| sitescore-app.surge.sh | $0 | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| pagescorer.surge.sh | $0 | Yes | Yes | Partial | No |

the one real gap is historical data. the paid tools store your scores over time so you can see trends. my free tools give you a point-in-time snapshot.

for most use cases, point-in-time is enough. you run the audit, you fix the issues, you run it again. you don't need a $99/month subscription to do that.

## where the CORS wall hits you

browser-based tools have one real constraint: CORS. you can't directly fetch arbitrary URLs from client-side JavaScript because browsers block cross-origin requests without the right headers.

three ways around this:

1. use the PageSpeed API (it fetches the URL server-side, no CORS issue)
2. use a CORS proxy (allorigins.win or cors-anywhere for development)
3. ask users to paste their HTML into a textarea

i use options 1 and 2 in combination. the PageSpeed API handles performance data. allorigins.win handles HTML scraping for on-page checks. neither requires a server i have to maintain.

the tradeoff: allorigins.win is a public proxy with rate limits and occasional downtime. if you're building something with real traffic, you'd want to run your own proxy. for a free tool, it's fine.

## the scoring formula

i wanted a single number, not just raw metrics. the scoring weights:

```javascript
function calculateScore(metrics) {
  const weights = {
    performance: 0.35,      // biggest user-facing impact
    seo: 0.25,              // second most important for discovery
    accessibility: 0.20,   // legal risk if ignored
    bestPractices: 0.20    // security + modern standards
  };

  return Math.round(
    metrics.performance * weights.performance +
    metrics.seo * weights.seo +
    metrics.accessibility * weights.accessibility +
    metrics.bestPractices * weights.bestPractices
  );
}
```

weights are opinionated. performance gets 35% because a slow site loses users before they read anything. tweak the weights for your audience. an ecommerce tool might weight performance at 50%.

## who uses these tools

three groups find them useful:

web developers who want a quick sanity check before handing a site to a client. they don't need a $99/month tool for a 5-minute check.

small business owners who can't afford GTmetrix. they heard their site is slow and want to see the numbers before hiring anyone.

freelancers who use audit results to sell fixes. run the audit on a prospect's site, screenshot the low scores, send it in a cold email. the tool paid for itself the first time you close a $500 fix.

## the actual build time

pagescorer.surge.sh took 4 hours to build from scratch. that includes the PageSpeed API integration, the on-page analysis, the scoring formula, and the UI.

sitescore-app.surge.sh took 2 hours because i reused the core from pagescorer and swapped the UI.

shopmetrics-pro.surge.sh took 3 hours because ecommerce checks require additional logic (schema markup detection, product page structure, checkout page analysis).

total: 9 hours of work. $0 recurring cost. tools that compete with products charging $99/month.

the lesson isn't that SaaS is dead. it's that most of the value in audit SaaS tools is in the UI, the reports, and the historical tracking, not in the analysis logic. the analysis logic is public APIs and DOM parsing.

build the parts that require a server. skip the parts that don't.
